episode_meta.title: Fabian Heckenberg, Naja Nielsen & Gard Steiro: The Hard Truths About AI Every Newsroom Leader Can't Ignore

guests.name: Fabian Heckenberger
guests.role: Managing Editor and Senior Editor for AI

ai_tools: AI agents for error-checking outputs (VG — one algorithm performs a task, another agent checks for mistakes)
AI lab / experimentation framework (Schibsted AI lab)
Interdisciplinary product teams with embedded editorial (SZ)

use_cases.description: VGX project: developing a new AI-powered news product for younger users, tested on recruited non-users rather than existing subscribers
use_cases.outcome: Learned that testing new products on super users gives misleading feedback; must recruit new target users for testing

use_cases.description: Using AI agents to check each other's outputs — one algorithm performs a content task, a second agent audits the output for errors
use_cases.outcome: Reduces risk of mistakes in automated content processes without requiring human-in-the-loop for every step

use_cases.description: Personalization of news content using AI to make journalism more relevant to individual users
use_cases.outcome: Recognized as key to building trust and relevance, especially with younger audiences; acknowledged as impossible with human-in-the-loop at scale

use_cases.description: Interdisciplinary product teams with embedded journalists from the newsroom working on homepage, apps, and audio products
use_cases.tool_used: Interdisciplinary team structure (not a specific AI tool)
use_cases.outcome: Established seven to eight years ago; now iterating to incorporate AI management into the same teams

use_cases.description: Scenario planning for AI strategy — working with multiple scenarios for how AI could develop over the next years and experimenting in different directions simultaneously
use_cases.tool_used: Strategic framework (not a specific AI tool)
use_cases.outcome: Enables agility under uncertainty; fosters open internal debate about AI futures

use_cases.description: Audio as a growing content format enriching the subscriber model beyond text articles
use_cases.outcome: Identified as a major growth area to supplement the subscriber business model

use_cases.description: INLAB survey on trust in journalism among young users in Norway and Sweden
use_cases.outcome: Found that younger audiences value relevance as the top driver of trust in news brands, more than transparency alone

use_cases.description: Yle (Finland) testing differently curated news using AI to move beyond one-size-fits-all editorial curation
use_cases.outcome: Described as 'first brave steps' in testing personalized news curation aligned to editorial values

key_claims.claim: If we still insist on humans in the loop for everything, we won't be able to take the full advantages of AI. It is impossible to personalize content with a human in the loop.
key_claims.speaker: Gard Steiro

key_claims.claim: Relevance was the number one reason young users gave for trusting a news brand, based on an INLAB survey of young users in Norway and Sweden.
key_claims.speaker: Gard Steiro

key_claims.claim: All the tools we are presenting here will be off the shelf in a year. What we need to focus on is what really can be our unique selling point for the future — journalism that can't be copied and gives value to readers.
key_claims.speaker: Gard Steiro

key_claims.claim: The worst legacy in media, especially news organizations, is the belief that the editor-in-chief must be the most clever person in the room and must make all decisions of importance.
key_claims.speaker: Naja Nielsen

key_claims.claim: People are not in doubt that we are competent. They are in doubt about whether we want to do them well, whether we really have their interests at our hearts.
key_claims.speaker: Naja Nielsen

key_claims.claim: In a world where it's so easy to create multimodal content and it's getting harder to verify if content is true, there's a chance for quality journalism to double down on what we already did over the last five to ten years.
key_claims.speaker: Fabian Heckenberger

key_claims.claim: News articles are maybe already dead — maybe next year — and you have to think in categories and in phases about which article types to sunset first.
key_claims.speaker: Fabian Heckenberger

key_claims.claim: If we don't think about new business models for journalism in the AI shift, then the products can be as good as we hope for, but we will not be able to earn enough money to finance journalism.
key_claims.speaker: Gard Steiro

key_claims.claim: Most media companies in the Nordic market are now owned by foundations, and that may be part of the future model for financing quality journalism.
key_claims.speaker: Gard Steiro

key_claims.claim: VG accidentally killed a Norwegian queen on their front page (she is still alive) — and that error was made by a human, not AI.
key_claims.speaker: Gard Steiro

key_claims.claim: The Erling Haaland being 'shot' misreport was an AI misaction on a photo shoot — and VG's sports section noted similar errors have happened a dozen times over twenty years without any Gen AI involvement.
key_claims.speaker: Fabian Heckenberger

key_claims.claim: There is no guarantee that public service broadcasting will continue in Europe — it is a live political debate everywhere.
key_claims.speaker: Naja Nielsen

challenges.description: Strategizing for AI when the future direction, regulation, and technology trajectory are all uncertain — leaders must make decisions with roughly 49% chance of being right
Prioritization battle between building features for current subscribers, testing new Gen AI features, and developing new tools — no clear framework for navigating these competing demands
Newsrooms are reluctant to integrate business development thinking into editorial AI projects, creating a gap between product innovation and revenue sustainability
Testing new products on existing loyal super users produces misleading feedback (selection bias) — they resist change and prefer the current experience
Different parts of the organization (tech, product, editorial, business) don't understand each other, making interdisciplinary AI collaboration difficult
Human-in-the-loop becomes a bottleneck that prevents taking full advantage of AI capabilities, especially for personalization at scale
Facts are not copyrightable, so when AI can generate content from facts, the traditional business model of news is threatened — articles are copyrighted but their factual content is not
The article format, largely unchanged for 25 years in digital, is an increasingly poor fit for younger audiences who prefer audio, video, conversation, or personalized formats
Incremental improvements to existing products for loyal users may not be radical enough to attract the next generation of news consumers
Ad revenue is threatened, especially for smaller players, and the two-pillar business model (ads plus subscriptions) may not be sustainable
Public service broadcasters face political pressure to justify their existence if they primarily serve older demographics and fail to reach younger audiences
Journalists are good at finding errors in other organizations but poor at admitting their own errors, undermining transparency and trust

quotes.text: I think my most important task as a leader is not to point direction, but to foster a discussion within the organization.
quotes.speaker: Gard Steiro

quotes.text: The worst legacy we've got in media, especially in news organizations, is that we believe the one that is the editor in chief must be the most clever man in the room and therefore he or she must make all decisions of importance.
quotes.speaker: Naja Nielsen

quotes.text: People are not in doubt that we are competent. They are in doubt about whether we want to do them well. Whether we really have their interests at our hearts and also whether our heart is in the right place because they see so much bullshit around.
quotes.speaker: Naja Nielsen

quotes.text: All the tools we are presenting here will be from the shelf in a year. So maybe we shouldn't focus so much on that and just wait. What we need to focus on is what really can be our unique selling point for the future.
quotes.speaker: Gard Steiro

quotes.text: In the start of AI we insisted on humans in loop. And if we still insist on that, we won't take the advantages of AI. It is impossible to have a personalized content with a human in loop.
quotes.speaker: Gard Steiro
quotes.topic: Moving beyond human-in-the-loop as a default

quotes.text: I started in journalism 25 years ago and I still write my stories the exactly the same way. So the article will die, should die, storytelling won't die.
quotes.speaker: Gard Steiro
quotes.topic: The death of the article format versus survival of storytelling

recommendations.advice: Focus on possibilities rather than threats — you can focus on threats all day, but focusing on possibilities gives you a chance to survive and thrive
recommendations.speaker: Gard Steiro

recommendations.advice: Foster open disagreement and discussion within the organization rather than trying to point a single direction; use scenario planning to explore multiple futures simultaneously
recommendations.speaker: Gard Steiro

recommendations.advice: Set short, clear timelines for AI projects (e.g., one year) with defined problems to solve; shut down projects that don't show progress, extend funding for those that do
recommendations.speaker: Gard Steiro

recommendations.advice: When testing new AI-powered products for new audiences, recruit users you don't currently have rather than testing on loyal super users, who will always prefer the status quo
recommendations.speaker: Gard Steiro

recommendations.advice: Create a transformative framework that gives the newsroom guidelines on what is happening and what is still unknown, while creating freedom and spaces for testing
recommendations.speaker: Fabian Heckenberger

recommendations.advice: Learn from Silicon Valley's way of working (Amazon, Netflix-style rapid iteration and testing) — copy their methods even if you disagree with their values
recommendations.speaker: Naja Nielsen

recommendations.advice: Include the public in product development, not just as passive audiences but as participants — move from broadcasting to co-creation
recommendations.speaker: Naja Nielsen
recommendations.target_audience: public service broadcasters and newsroom leaders

recommendations.advice: Measure ROI across three baskets: (1) subscriber retention and subscriber views, (2) efficiency gains (hours saved), and (3) experimental testing — but phase out the pure experimentation basket as the field matures
recommendations.speaker: Fabian Heckenberger

recommendations.advice: Identify the journalism your organization does that truly cannot be copied and gives unique value to readers, then move resources fast into that area
recommendations.speaker: Gard Steiro

recommendations.advice: Think about killing the article format in phases — start with news articles which may already be obsolete, while preserving long-form reports, investigative pieces, and opinion
recommendations.speaker: Fabian Heckenberger

recommendations.advice: Every AI project needs a clear business value and business creation component — newsrooms must overcome their reluctance to work on business strategy alongside editorial innovation
recommendations.speaker: Gard Steiro

recommendations.advice: Use multi-agent systems where one AI performs a task and another AI agent checks for errors, rather than relying solely on human-in-the-loop for quality assurance
recommendations.speaker: Gard Steiro

recommendations.advice: Explore smaller, diversified revenue streams rather than searching for a single unicorn business model; consider foundation ownership as a financing model for quality journalism
recommendations.speaker: Gard Steiro

external_references: Schibsted AI lab — AI experimentation lab used by VG
VGX project — VG's AI-powered news product for younger users
INLAB — research partnership that surveyed young users in Norway and Sweden on trust in journalism
Yle (Finland) — Finnish public broadcaster testing differently curated news
SR (Sveriges Radio) — Swedish Radio, referenced for transparency approach to AI development
Nordic AI and Media Summit 2025 — live event where the panel was recorded
Casper — referenced as organizer/curator of the summit program
Eri from Helsingin Sanomat — referenced as saying it is leaders' responsibility to be informed about AI
David Caswell — credited with coining the phrase 'digital/online is the new print'
Amazon and Netflix — cited as Silicon Valley models for working methods news organizations should emulate
Erling Haaland — footballer whose 'shooting' was misreported due to AI misaction on a photo shoot
Lea Kosko from Schibsted — audience member who asked about the death of the article format
Johan Ekman from Aftonbladet — audience member who asked about sustainable business models
Eric Scherer from France Television — audience member who asked about AI reducing redundant event coverage (Pope funeral example)
Melodifestivalen — referenced as example of shared cultural events people consume together
Harvard Innovation Labs — Spark Grant supporting the podcast
